message:
fix(db): route schema migrations through canonical SQLite to prevent index corruption

Legacy fixtures with pre-existing tables were experiencing malformed-index
errors when schema DDL was applied through the Franken SQLite connection path.
The root cause was that FrankenSQLite's connection handling could leave index
structures in an inconsistent state when applying broad CREATE TABLE IF NOT
EXISTS / ALTER TABLE migrations on databases that already had data.

Both the CLI sync path (open_db_sync_with_database_url) and the async pool
init path (run_sqlite_init_once) now run schema migrations through canonical
sqlmodel_sqlite::SqliteConnection, then reopen through the Franken path for
runtime traffic. This separation ensures migration DDL uses battle-tested
SQLite semantics while runtime queries still benefit from Franken's
performance characteristics.

The CLI path adds init_schema_sqlite_canonical() which opens via canonical
SQLite, applies base PRAGMAs and schema init, then closes before reopening
through the Franken fallback path. Error recovery still triggers on
corruption signatures.

The pool path restructures run_sqlite_init_once() to use
open_sqlite_file_with_lock_retry_canonical() for migrations, made possible
by generalizing open_sqlite_file_with_lock_retry_impl over the connection
type parameter.

Backup candidate discovery now recognizes timestamped .bak.YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS
files and prioritizes them over .backup-* series, since the .bak timestamp
series is the most recent backup format.

Includes integration tests verifying that legacy fixture rows survive the
init process in both CLI and pool paths, plus backup candidate ordering tests.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
